title: Top view of tree
description: Print the nodes visible from the top view of a binary tree

examples.explanation: Step 1: Initialize queue with (root=1, HD=0), map={}. Step 2: Process (1,0): HD=0 not in map, add map[0]=1. Enqueue left child 2 with HD=-1, right child 3 with HD=1. Queue: [(2,-1), (3,1)], map={0:1}. Step 3: Process (2,-1): HD=-1 not in map, add map[-1]=2. Enqueue children 4 with HD=-2, 5 with HD=0. Queue: [(3,1), (4,-2), (5,0)], map={0:1, -1:2}. Step 4: Process (3,1): HD=1 not in map, add map[1]=3. Enqueue children 6 with HD=0, 7 with HD=2. Queue: [(4,-2), (5,0), (6,0), (7,2)], map={0:1, -1:2, 1:3}. Step 5: Process (4,-2): HD=-2 not in map, add map[-2]=4. No children. Queue: [(5,0), (6,0), (7,2)], map={0:1, -1:2, 1:3, -2:4}. Step 6: Process (5,0): HD=0 already in map, skip. No children. Queue: [(6,0), (7,2)], map unchanged. Step 7: Process (6,0): HD=0 already in map, skip. No children. Queue: [(7,2)], map unchanged. Step 8: Process (7,2): HD=2 not in map, add map[2]=7. No children. Queue empty. Step 9: Sort map keys: [-2, -1, 0, 1, 2]. Output values: 4 2 1 3 7.

Step 1: Initialize queue with (1,0), map={}. Step 2: Process (1,0): map[0]=1. Enqueue (2,-1), (3,1). Queue: [(2,-1), (3,1)], map={0:1}. Step 3: Process (2,-1): map[-1]=2. Enqueue right child 4 with HD=0. Queue: [(3,1), (4,0)], map={0:1, -1:2}. Step 4: Process (3,1): map[1]=3. Enqueue left child 5 with HD=0. Queue: [(4,0), (5,0)], map={0:1, -1:2, 1:3}. Step 5: Process (4,0): HD=0 already in map, skip. No children. Queue: [(5,0)], map unchanged. Step 6: Process (5,0): HD=0 already in map, skip. No children. Queue empty. Step 7: Sort keys: [-1, 0, 1]. Output: 2 1 3.

approaches.name: Approach 1: Level Order Traversal with Horizontal Distance Tracking
approaches.summary: Perform a level-order traversal (BFS) of the binary tree, tracking the horizontal distance (HD) of each node from the root. For each HD, store the first node encountered at that distance to capture the top view. Use a queue for traversal and a map to store HD-node pairs.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Top View of Binary Tree using BFS with Horizontal Distance
ladders.desc: Complete solution to print nodes visible from the top view of a binary tree using level-order traversal with horizontal distance tracking.
ladders.explanation: The top view of a binary tree shows the nodes that would be visible when looking from above, where nodes at the same horizontal position are hidden behind the topmost node. The key insight is that for each horizontal distance from the root, we need the first node encountered at that distance during a level-order traversal. This ensures we capture the highest visible node at each horizontal position. We perform a BFS traversal using a queue, tracking each node's horizontal distance (HD) where root has HD=0, left child has HD-1, and right child has HD+1. We maintain a map (dictionary) to store the first node value for each HD encountered. Since BFS processes nodes level by level, the first node at a particular HD will be the highest (closest to root) node at that position. After traversal, we output nodes sorted by HD to get left-to-right order. Time complexity is O(n) for visiting all nodes, and space complexity is O(n) for the queue and map. This approach works because BFS ensures we process nodes in increasing depth order, guaranteeing we capture the topmost node at each horizontal position before any nodes below it at the same position.

examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize queue with (root=1, HD=0), map={}. Step 2: Process (1,0): HD=0 not in map, add map[0]=1. Enqueue left child 2 with HD=-1, right child 3 with HD=1. Queue: [(2,-1), (3,1)], map={0:1}. Step 3: Process (2,-1): HD=-1 not in map, add map[-1]=2. Enqueue children 4 with HD=-2, 5 with HD=0. Queue: [(3,1), (4,-2), (5,0)], map={0:1, -1:2}. Step 4: Process (3,1): HD=1 not in map, add map[1]=3. Enqueue children 6 with HD=0, 7 with HD=2. Queue: [(4,-2), (5,0), (6,0), (7,2)], map={0:1, -1:2, 1:3}. Step 5: Process (4,-2): HD=-2 not in map, add map[-2]=4. No children. Queue: [(5,0), (6,0), (7,2)], map={0:1, -1:2, 1:3, -2:4}. Step 6: Process (5,0): HD=0 already in map, skip. No children. Queue: [(6,0), (7,2)], map unchanged. Step 7: Process (6,0): HD=0 already in map, skip. No children. Queue: [(7,2)], map unchanged. Step 8: Process (7,2): HD=2 not in map, add map[2]=7. No children. Queue empty. Step 9: Sort map keys: [-2, -1, 0, 1, 2]. Output values: 4 2 1 3 7.

Step 1: Initialize queue with (1,0), map={}. Step 2: Process (1,0): map[0]=1. Enqueue (2,-1), (3,1). Queue: [(2,-1), (3,1)], map={0:1}. Step 3: Process (2,-1): map[-1]=2. Enqueue right child 4 with HD=0. Queue: [(3,1), (4,0)], map={0:1, -1:2}. Step 4: Process (3,1): map[1]=3. Enqueue left child 5 with HD=0. Queue: [(4,0), (5,0)], map={0:1, -1:2, 1:3}. Step 5: Process (4,0): HD=0 already in map, skip. No children. Queue: [(5,0)], map unchanged. Step 6: Process (5,0): HD=0 already in map, skip. No children. Queue empty. Step 7: Sort keys: [-1, 0, 1]. Output: 2 1 3.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — BFS Traversal with Horizontal Distance Mapping
ladders.desc: Perform level-order traversal while tracking horizontal distances and storing first occurrence in a map.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine implements the core traversal logic for the top view problem. The intuition is that BFS processes nodes level by level, ensuring we encounter nodes in order of increasing depth. By tracking horizontal distance (HD) and storing only the first node at each HD, we guarantee capturing the topmost node at each horizontal position. The algorithm uses a queue to store (node, HD) pairs. Starting with the root at HD=0, we dequeue a pair, check if its HD is already in the map—if not, we store the node's value. Then we enqueue left child with HD-1 and right child with HD+1. This propagates HD values through the tree. The map acts as a lookup to avoid overwriting with deeper nodes. Time complexity is O(n) as each node is processed once. Space complexity is O(n) for the queue and map. This feeds into the full problem by producing the map of HD to node values, which then needs sorting and output. Common mistakes include not using BFS (DFS would give wrong order), forgetting to check map before storing, or incorrect HD propagation. The BFS order is crucial because it ensures we process all nodes at depth d before any at depth d+1.

examples.trace: Step 1: Queue=[(1,0)], map={}. Step 2: Process (1,0): HD=0 not in map, map[0]=1. Enqueue (2,-1), (3,1). Queue=[(2,-1),(3,1)]. Step 3: Process (2,-1): map[-1]=2. Enqueue (4,-2), (5,0). Queue=[(3,1),(4,-2),(5,0)]. Step 4: Process (3,1): map[1]=3. Enqueue (6,0), (7,2). Queue=[(4,-2),(5,0),(6,0),(7,2)]. Step 5: Process (4,-2): map[-2]=4. No children. Queue=[(5,0),(6,0),(7,2)]. Step 6: Process (5,0): HD=0 in map, skip. Queue=[(6,0),(7,2)]. Step 7: Process (6,0): HD=0 in map, skip. Queue=[(7,2)]. Step 8: Process (7,2): map[2]=7. Queue empty. Final map: {-2:4, -1:2, 0:1, 1:3, 2:7}.

Step 1: Queue=[(1,0)], map={}. Step 2: Process (1,0): map[0]=1. Enqueue (2,-1), (3,1). Queue=[(2,-1),(3,1)]. Step 3: Process (2,-1): map[-1]=2. Enqueue right child (4,0). Queue=[(3,1),(4,0)]. Step 4: Process (3,1): map[1]=3. Enqueue left child (5,0). Queue=[(4,0),(5,0)]. Step 5: Process (4,0): HD=0 in map, skip. Queue=[(5,0)]. Step 6: Process (5,0): HD=0 in map, skip. Queue empty. Final map: {-1:2, 0:1, 1:3}.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Horizontal Distance Concept and First-Encounter Capture
ladders.desc: Understand how horizontal distance determines top visibility and why first encounter in BFS matters.
ladders.explanation: The core logic revolves around two key ideas: horizontal distance (HD) and the first-encounter principle in BFS. HD is a coordinate system where the root is at position 0, moving left subtracts 1, and moving right adds 1. This creates a one-dimensional axis representing horizontal positions in the tree. For the top view, at each HD, only the node closest to the root (highest in the tree) should be visible—nodes below it at the same HD are hidden. BFS processes nodes level by level, so the first time we encounter a particular HD, it must be the highest node at that position. Subsequent nodes at the same HD are deeper and thus ignored. This works because BFS guarantees we visit all nodes at depth d before any at depth d+1. The algorithm leverages this by using a map to store the first node value for each HD. When we process a node, we check if its HD is already in the map; if not, we add it. This ensures only the topmost node is stored. Time complexity for this logic is O(1) per node check, and space is O(w) for the map where w is the width of HD range. This feeds into the sub-routine by providing the rule for what to store during traversal. Common mistakes include using DFS instead of BFS (which might not guarantee first encounter is highest), or storing all nodes and then trying to find minimum depth per HD (inefficient). The intuition is that BFS order naturally gives us nodes in increasing depth, making first encounter sufficient.

examples.input: HD values for tree: root=1 (HD=0), left child=2 (HD=-1), right child=3 (HD=1). BFS order: 1,2,3.
examples.trace: Step 1: Process node 1 at HD=0. First time seeing HD=0, store 1. Step 2: Process node 2 at HD=-1. First time seeing HD=-1, store 2. Step 3: Process node 3 at HD=1. First time seeing HD=1, store 3. No other nodes, so these are the top nodes at each HD.

examples.input: Tree with overlap: root=1 (HD=0), left=2 (HD=-1), right=3 (HD=1), left's right=4 (HD=0). BFS order: 1,2,3,4.
examples.trace: Step 1: Process 1 at HD=0: first encounter, store 1. Step 2: Process 2 at HD=-1: first encounter, store 2. Step 3: Process 3 at HD=1: first encounter, store 3. Step 4: Process 4 at HD=0: HD=0 already stored (with node 1), so skip. Node 4 is deeper than node 1 at same HD, so hidden in top view.

ladders.title: Building Block — Level-Order Traversal (BFS) on Binary Tree
ladders.desc: Implement BFS to visit all nodes in a binary tree level by level.
ladders.explanation: Level-order traversal, also known as Breadth-First Search (BFS), visits nodes of a binary tree level by level from top to bottom and left to right. The intuition is to use a queue data structure: start by enqueuing the root, then repeatedly dequeue a node, process it, and enqueue its children (left then right). This ensures nodes are processed in order of increasing depth. The algorithm works because the queue maintains the order of discovery; nodes at depth d are dequeued before any nodes at depth d+1. Pseudocode: initialize queue with root; while queue not empty: node = dequeue(); process(node); if node.left exists: enqueue(node.left); if node.right exists: enqueue(node.right). Time complexity is O(n) as each node is enqueued and dequeued once. Space complexity is O(w) where w is the maximum width of the tree (number of nodes at the deepest level), which can be O(n) in worst-case. This feeds into the core logic by providing the traversal order needed for first-encounter capture. Common mistakes include using a stack instead of queue (which gives DFS), forgetting to check for null children, or not handling empty tree. BFS is fundamental for problems requiring level-wise processing.

examples.trace: Step 1: Queue=[1]. Dequeue 1, process 1. Enqueue left child 2, right child 3. Queue=[2,3]. Step 2: Dequeue 2, process 2. No children. Queue=[3]. Step 3: Dequeue 3, process 3. No children. Queue empty. Order: 1,2,3.

Step 1: Queue=[1]. Dequeue 1, process 1. Enqueue 2,3. Queue=[2,3]. Step 2: Dequeue 2, process 2. Enqueue 4,5. Queue=[3,4,5]. Step 3: Dequeue 3, process 3. Enqueue 6,7. Queue=[4,5,6,7]. Step 4: Dequeue 4, process 4. No children. Queue=[5,6,7]. Step 5: Dequeue 5, process 5. No children. Queue=[6,7]. Step 6: Dequeue 6, process 6. No children. Queue=[7]. Step 7: Dequeue 7, process 7. Queue empty. Order: 1,2,3,4,5,6,7.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Queue Operations for Tree Traversal
ladders.desc: Use a queue to manage nodes in FIFO order for level-by-level processing.
ladders.explanation: A queue is a First-In-First-Out (FIFO) data structure essential for BFS traversal. The intuition is that we want to process nodes in the order they are discovered: root first, then its children, then grandchildren, etc. A queue supports enqueue (add to back) and dequeue (remove from front) operations. For tree traversal, we enqueue the root, then repeatedly dequeue a node, process it, and enqueue its children. This ensures nodes at the same level are processed before moving deeper. Pseudocode: queue = []; enqueue(root); while queue not empty: node = dequeue(); process(node); if node.left: enqueue(node.left); if node.right: enqueue(node.right). Time complexity per operation is O(1) for enqueue and dequeue if using a linked list or deque. Space complexity is O(n) for storing nodes in the queue. This feeds into BFS by providing the mechanism to traverse levels. Common mistakes include using a list without efficient dequeue (O(n) time), not checking for empty queue, or enqueuing null nodes. The queue ensures proper order: imagine a line where the first person served is the first to arrive.

examples.input:
Tree: 1
     /   \
    2     3
Queue operations start with root 1.
examples.trace: Step 1: Enqueue root 1. Queue=[1]. Step 2: Dequeue 1, process it. Enqueue left child 2, right child 3. Queue=[2,3]. Step 3: Dequeue 2, process it. No children to enqueue. Queue=[3]. Step 4: Dequeue 3, process it. Queue empty.

examples.input:
Tree: 1
       /
      2
     /
    3
Queue operations start with root 1.
examples.trace: Step 1: Enqueue 1. Queue=[1]. Step 2: Dequeue 1, process it. Enqueue left child 2. Queue=[2]. Step 3: Dequeue 2, process it. Enqueue left child 3. Queue=[3]. Step 4: Dequeue 3, process it. Queue empty.

testCases.input: Enqueue A, dequeue A, enqueue B,C, dequeue B, enqueue D, dequeue C, dequeue D
Enqueue 10, dequeue, enqueue 20,30, dequeue 20, enqueue 40, dequeue 30, enqueue 50, dequeue 40, dequeue 50
Enqueue X, dequeue X, no enqueue
Enqueue 1, dequeue, enqueue 2, dequeue, enqueue 3, dequeue
Enqueue A,B,C, dequeue A, dequeue B, dequeue C
Enqueue 5, dequeue, enqueue 6,7, dequeue 6, enqueue 8, dequeue 7, enqueue 9, dequeue 8, dequeue 9
Enqueue 100, dequeue, enqueue 200, dequeue, enqueue 300, dequeue
Enqueue P, dequeue, enqueue Q,R, dequeue Q, enqueue S, dequeue R, enqueue T, dequeue S, dequeue T
Enqueue 1, dequeue, enqueue 2,3,4, dequeue 2, dequeue 3, dequeue 4

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Binary Tree Node Structure and Child Access
ladders.desc: Understand how a binary tree node is represented and how to access its left and right children.
ladders.explanation: A binary tree is a hierarchical data structure where each node has at most two children: left and right. Each node typically contains a value and references (pointers) to its left and right children. In code, this is often represented as a class with attributes: value, left, right. The left and right attributes may be null (or None) if the child does not exist. The intuition is that starting from the root, we can navigate the tree by following these references. For example, to access the left child of a node, we use node.left; to access the right child, node.right. This foundational concept is crucial for any tree traversal. Pseudocode: class Node: def __init__(self, val): self.val = val; self.left = None; self.right = None. To build a tree: root = Node(1); root.left = Node(2); root.right = Node(3). Time and space are O(1) for accessing children. This feeds into queue operations by providing the nodes to enqueue. Common mistakes include not checking if a child exists before accessing it (leading to null pointer errors), or misunderstanding the tree structure. Understanding this allows beginners to grasp how trees are built and traversed.

examples.input: Create a node with value 10, left child 20, right child 30.
examples.trace: Step 1: Create root node: node1 = Node(10). Step 2: Create left child: node2 = Node(20). Step 3: Create right child: node3 = Node(30). Step 4: Set references: node1.left = node2; node1.right = node3. Now node1 has val=10, left points to node2 (val=20), right points to node3 (val=30).

examples.input: Given root node with val=5, left child val=3, right child null. Access children.
examples.trace: Step 1: root is a Node with val=5. Step 2: root.left is a Node with val=3. Step 3: root.right is None (null). To check: if root.left is not None, we can access root.left.val which is 3. If we try to access root.right.val, it would cause an error because root.right is None.

testCases.input: Node val=2, left child val=1, right child val=3
Node val=10, left child val=5, right child null
Node val=7, left child null, right child val=9
Node val=100, left child val=50, right child val=150
Node val=0, left child val=-1, right child val=1
Node val=8, left child val=4, right child val=12
Node val=99, left child null, right child null
Node val=6, left child val=2, right child val=10

approaches.name: Approach 2: Preorder Traversal with Depth Comparison
approaches.summary: Use a preorder traversal (DFS) to visit nodes, tracking horizontal distance and depth. For each horizontal distance, store the node only if it has a smaller depth than any previously stored node at that distance, ensuring the topmost node is selected. Maintain a map to compare depths.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Top View of Binary Tree using Preorder Traversal with Depth Comparison
ladders.desc: Complete solution to print nodes visible from the top view of a binary tree using DFS with horizontal distance and depth tracking.
ladders.explanation: The top view of a binary tree shows the nodes that would be visible when looking from above, where if multiple nodes share the same horizontal position, only the topmost (closest to root) node is visible. This approach uses preorder traversal (DFS) to systematically visit all nodes while tracking two key metrics: horizontal distance (hd) and depth. Horizontal distance represents the column position relative to the root (root at 0, left child subtracts 1, right child adds 1). Depth represents the vertical distance from the root (root depth 0, each level adds 1). We maintain a dictionary (map) where keys are horizontal distances and values are pairs of (node value, depth). During traversal, for each node, we check its horizontal distance. If this distance hasn't been seen before, we store the node. If it has been seen, we compare depths - only update if the current node has a smaller depth (closer to root). This ensures we always keep the topmost node for each horizontal position. The algorithm starts at the root with hd=0 and depth=0, recursively traverses left (hd-1, depth+1) then right (hd+1, depth+1). After traversal, we extract nodes from the dictionary sorted by horizontal distance to get left-to-right order. Time complexity is O(n) since we visit each node once and dictionary operations are O(1). Space complexity is O(n) for the recursion stack (worst case skewed tree) and dictionary storage. Connection to L1: This integrates the depth comparison logic with full tree traversal. Common mistakes include not handling depth comparison correctly (should compare depths, not values), forgetting to sort output by horizontal distance, or using BFS unnecessarily when DFS works. Real code pattern: def topView(root): map = {}; def dfs(node, hd, depth): if not node: return; if hd not in map or depth < map[hd][1]: map[hd] = (node.val, depth); dfs(node.left, hd-1, depth+1); dfs(node.right, hd+1, depth+1); dfs(root, 0, 0); return [map[hd][0] for hd in sorted(map.keys())].

examples.input: Tree: 1 (root), left: 2, right: 3, 2's left: 4, 2's right: 5, 3's left: 6, 3's right: 7
examples.trace: Step 1: Start at root 1, hd=0, depth=0. Map empty, store map[0]=(1,0). Step 2: Traverse left to node 2, hd=-1, depth=1. Map[-1] empty, store (2,1). Step 3: From 2, traverse left to node 4, hd=-2, depth=2. Store (4,2). Step 4: From 4, left/right null return. Step 5: Back to 2, traverse right to node 5, hd=0, depth=2. Map[0] exists with (1,0). Compare depths: 2 > 0, so keep (1,0). Step 6: Back to 1, traverse right to node 3, hd=1, depth=1. Store (3,1). Step 7: From 3, left to node 6, hd=0, depth=2. Map[0] has (1,0), 2>0, keep (1,0). Step 8: From 3, right to node 7, hd=2, depth=2. Store (7,2). Step 9: Traversal complete. Map: {-2:(4,2), -1:(2,1), 0:(1,0), 1:(3,1), 2:(7,2)}. Sort hd: -2,-1,0,1,2. Extract values: 4,2,1,3,7.

examples.input: Tree: 1 (root), left: 2, right: 3, 2's left: 4, 2's right: null, 4's left: 5, 4's right: null
examples.trace: Step 1: Root 1, hd=0, depth=0, store map[0]=(1,0). Step 2: Left to 2, hd=-1, depth=1, store (2,1). Step 3: Left to 4, hd=-2, depth=2, store (4,2). Step 4: Left to 5, hd=-3, depth=3, store (5,3). Step 5: Back to 4, right null. Step 6: Back to 2, right null. Step 7: Back to 1, right to 3, hd=1, depth=1, store (3,1). Step 8: Map: {-3:(5,3), -2:(4,2), -1:(2,1), 0:(1,0), 1:(3,1)}. Sort hd: -3,-2,-1,0,1. Values: 5,4,2,1,3.

testCases.input: Tree: 1, left: 2, right: null, 2's left: 3, right: null, 3's left: 4
Tree: 1, left: 2, right: 3, 2's left: 4, 2's right: 5, 4's left: 6, 5's right: 7
Tree: 10, left: 20, right: 30, 20's left: 40, 20's right: 60, 30's left: 90, 30's right: 100
Tree: 1, left: 2, right: 3, 2's left: 4, 2's right: 5, 3's left: 6, 3's right: 7, 4's left: 8, 7's right: 9

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Depth Comparison in Preorder Traversal for Horizontal Distances
ladders.desc: Implement the DFS traversal that visits nodes and updates a map based on horizontal distance and depth comparison.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the heart of the approach: a recursive preorder traversal function that takes a node, its horizontal distance (hd), and depth as parameters. The function's purpose is to visit each node exactly once and decide whether to store it in a global map for its horizontal distance. The intuition is that during DFS, we encounter nodes in a root-first order, but depth comparison ensures we select the shallowest node for each horizontal position. At each node, we check if its hd is already in the map. If not, we store the node's value and depth. If yes, we compare the current node's depth with the stored depth - if current depth is smaller (closer to root), we update the map; otherwise, we skip. Then we recursively call the function for left child (with hd-1, depth+1) and right child (with hd+1, depth+1). The base case is when node is null. Time complexity for this sub-routine is O(n) as it visits all n nodes. Space complexity is O(h) for recursion stack, where h is tree height. Connection to L0: This function is called from the main solution with root parameters. Connection to L2: This implements the core logic of depth comparison. Common mistakes: Forgetting to pass updated depth/hd to children, incorrect comparison operator (should be < for depth), or not using a global/mutable map. Real code pattern: def dfs(node, hd, depth, map): if not node: return; if hd not in map or depth < map[hd][1]: map[hd] = (node.val, depth); dfs(node.left, hd-1, depth+1, map); dfs(node.right, hd+1, depth+1, map).

examples.input: Node: 1 (root), hd=0, depth=0, map={}. Then traverse to left child 2 with hd=-1, depth=1
examples.trace: Step 1: Call dfs(1,0,0,map). Node not null. hd=0 not in map, store map[0]=(1,0). Step 2: Call dfs(left of 1, i.e., node 2, hd=0-1=-1, depth=0+1=1, map). Step 3: In dfs(2,-1,1,map), node not null. hd=-1 not in map, store map[-1]=(2,1). Step 4: Continue traversal from node 2.

examples.input: Node: 3 with hd=1, depth=2, map already has {1: (10,1)} from previous node
examples.trace: Step 1: Call dfs(3,1,2,map). Node not null. Step 2: Check hd=1 in map? Yes, current stored is (10,1) with depth 1. Step 3: Compare current depth 2 with stored depth 1: 2 < 1? False. Step 4: Do not update map. Step 5: Continue traversal.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Selecting Topmost Node by Comparing Depths for Same Horizontal Distance
ladders.desc: Understand and implement the logic to choose the node with minimum depth for each horizontal distance.
ladders.explanation: The core insight is that for the top view, among all nodes at the same horizontal distance (same vertical line), only the one with the smallest depth (closest to root) should be visible. This is because when viewing from above, nodes higher in the tree obscure those below. We implement this by maintaining a data structure (like a dictionary) that maps horizontal distances to (node value, depth) pairs. When we encounter a node at a particular horizontal distance, we check if this distance is already in our map. If not, we add the node. If it is, we compare the depth of the current node with the stored depth. If current depth is strictly less than stored depth, we update the map with the new node; otherwise, we ignore it. This ensures the map always holds the shallowest node seen so far for each horizontal distance. The intuition: as we traverse the tree, we might encounter multiple nodes at the same horizontal position (e.g., a node and its descendant in the same column). By always keeping the one with minimum depth, we effectively select the topmost. Time complexity for this logic per node is O(1) for dictionary lookup/update. Space complexity is O(k) where k is number of unique horizontal distances (at most n). Connection to L1: This comparison is performed inside the DFS traversal. Connection to L3: This relies on tracking depth and horizontal distance. Common mistakes: Comparing node values instead of depths, using <= instead of < (if depths equal, first encountered should be kept, so no update), or not initializing properly. Real code pattern: if hd not in map: map[hd] = (node.val, depth); else: if depth < map[hd][1]: map[hd] = (node.val, depth).

examples.input: Horizontal distance = 0, current node depth = 2, map currently has {0: (5, 3)}
examples.trace: Step 1: Check hd=0 in map? Yes. Step 2: Retrieve stored depth: 3. Step 3: Compare current depth 2 with stored depth 3: 2 < 3 is true. Step 4: Update map[0] to (current node value, 2).

examples.input: Horizontal distance = -1, current node depth = 4, map currently has {-1: (7, 2)}
examples.trace: Step 1: Check hd=-1 in map? Yes. Step 2: Stored depth is 2. Step 3: Compare 4 < 2? False. Step 4: Do nothing, map unchanged.

ladders.title: Building Block — Tracking Horizontal Distance and Depth During Tree Traversal
ladders.desc: Learn to assign and update horizontal distance and depth values as you move through a binary tree.
ladders.explanation: In tree traversal, horizontal distance (hd) and depth are auxiliary values that help determine positional relationships. Horizontal distance measures how far left or right a node is relative to the root: root has hd=0, moving to left child subtracts 1, moving to right child adds 1. This creates a coordinate system where nodes in the same column have the same hd. Depth measures how many edges from the root: root depth=0, each child increases depth by 1. During traversal (like preorder), we start at root with hd=0, depth=0. When we go to a left child, we update hd = parent_hd - 1 and depth = parent_depth + 1. For right child, hd = parent_hd + 1, depth = parent_depth + 1. These values are passed as parameters in recursive calls. The intuition: hd tracks the x-coordinate, depth tracks y-coordinate in a 2D projection of the tree. This allows us to compare nodes spatially. Time and space are O(1) per node for updates. Connection to L2: These values are used in the depth comparison logic. Connection to L4: This builds on basic tree traversal. Common mistakes: Incorrectly updating hd (e.g., adding for left), not incrementing depth, or resetting values incorrectly. Real code pattern: For left child: dfs(node.left, hd-1, depth+1); for right child: dfs(node.right, hd+1, depth+1).

examples.input: Start at root with hd=0, depth=0. Move to left child, then to right child of that child.
examples.output: After moves: first child hd=-1,depth=1; second child hd=0,depth=2
examples.trace: Step 1: Root: hd=0, depth=0. Step 2: Move left: new hd = 0 - 1 = -1, new depth = 0 + 1 = 1. Step 3: From this node (hd=-1,depth=1), move right: new hd = -1 + 1 = 0, new depth = 1 + 1 = 2.

examples.input: Node at hd=2, depth=3. Move to its left child, then to left child again.
examples.output: First left: hd=1,depth=4; second left: hd=0,depth=5
examples.trace: Step 1: Current: hd=2, depth=3. Step 2: Move left: hd = 2 - 1 = 1, depth = 3 + 1 = 4. Step 3: Move left from here: hd = 1 - 1 = 0, depth = 4 + 1 = 5.

testCases.input: Start hd=1,depth=4, move left then right
testCases.expected: After left: hd=0,depth=5; after right: hd=1,depth=6

testCases.input: Start hd=0,depth=0, move right then left
testCases.expected: After right: hd=1,depth=1; after left: hd=0,depth=2

testCases.input: Start hd=-1,depth=3, move left then left
testCases.expected: After first left: hd=-2,depth=4; after second: hd=-3,depth=5

testCases.input: Start hd=2,depth=2, move right then right
testCases.expected: After first right: hd=3,depth=3; after second: hd=4,depth=4

testCases.input: Start hd=0,depth=5, move left then right then left
testCases.expected: After left: hd=-1,depth=6; after right: hd=0,depth=7; after left: hd=-1,depth=8

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Preorder Traversal (DFS) of a Binary Tree
ladders.desc: Implement depth-first search in preorder (root, left, right) to visit all nodes of a binary tree.
ladders.explanation: Preorder traversal is a fundamental DFS technique where you visit the root node first, then recursively traverse the left subtree, then the right subtree. For a binary tree, this means: process the current node, then call function on left child, then on right child. The intuition is to explore as deep as possible along each branch before backtracking, prioritizing the root before its children. This order is natural for problems where you need to process parent before children. Algorithm: define a function preorder(node): if node is null, return; else: visit(node); preorder(node.left); preorder(node.right). Time complexity is O(n) where n is number of nodes, as each node is visited once. Space complexity is O(h) in recursion stack, where h is tree height (O(n) worst for skewed tree). Connection to L3: This traversal provides the framework to which we add hd and depth tracking. Connection to L5: This builds on understanding tree nodes and recursion. Common mistakes: Forgetting base case (null check), swapping left/right order, or infinite recursion if tree has cycles (but binary trees are acyclic). Real code pattern: def preorder(node): if not node: return; print(node.val); preorder(node.left); preorder(node.right).

examples.trace: Step 1: Start at root 1, visit 1. Step 2: Call preorder on left child 2. Step 3: At node 2, visit 2. Step 4: Call preorder on left of 2 (null), returns. Step 5: Call preorder on right of 2 (null), returns. Step 6: Back to node 1, call preorder on right child 3. Step 7: At node 3, visit 3. Step 8: Call preorder on left/right of 3 (both null). Done.

Step 1: Visit root 1. Step 2: Traverse left subtree from 1: go to node 2, visit 2. Step 3: From 2, go left to 4, visit 4. Step 4: From 4, left/right null, back to 2. Step 5: From 2, go right to 5, visit 5. Step 6: Back to 1, traverse right subtree: go to 3, visit 3. Done.

testCases.input: Tree: 1, left: 2, right: 3, 2's left: 4, 2's right: 5, 4's left: 6
Tree: 10, left: 20, right: 30, 20's left: 40, 20's right: 50, 30's left: 60
Tree: A, left: B, right: C, B's left: D, B's right: E, C's left: F, C's right: G

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Binary Tree Nodes and Recursive Function Calls
ladders.desc: Understand the basic structure of a binary tree node and how to write a simple recursive function to traverse it.
ladders.explanation: A binary tree is a hierarchical data structure where each node has at most two children: left and right. Each node typically contains a value (e.g., integer) and references to its left and right children. In code, this is often represented as a class with attributes: val, left, right. A null reference indicates no child. Recursion is a natural way to process trees because a tree is recursively defined: a tree consists of a root node with left and right subtrees that are themselves trees. A recursive function calls itself with smaller subproblems. For trees, a base case is when node is null (empty tree). The recursive case processes the node and calls the function on its children. Intuition: Instead of manually tracking all nodes, recursion uses the call stack to manage traversal. Time/space analysis depends on implementation but generally O(n) time for visiting n nodes. Connection to L4: This foundation enables preorder traversal. Common mistakes: Not handling null nodes (leading to errors), infinite recursion if base case missing, or misunderstanding tree structure. Real code pattern: class Node: def __init__(self, val): self.val = val; self.left = None; self.right = None. Recursive example: def print_tree(node): if node is None: return; print(node.val); print_tree(node.left); print_tree(node.right).

examples.input: Node with val=5, left=Node(3), right=Node(7). Call print_tree on root.
examples.trace: Step 1: Call print_tree(root node 5). Node not null, print 5. Step 2: Call print_tree(left child node 3). Node not null, print 3. Step 3: Call print_tree(left of node 3): null, returns. Step 4: Call print_tree(right of node 3): null, returns. Step 5: Back to node 5, call print_tree(right child node 7). Print 7. Step 6: Call print_tree on left/right of node 7 (both null). Done.

examples.input: Node with val=1, left=null, right=Node(2). Call print_tree on root.
examples.trace: Step 1: Call print_tree(root 1). Not null, print 1. Step 2: Call print_tree(left child): null, returns. Step 3: Call print_tree(right child node 2). Not null, print 2. Step 4: Call print_tree on left/right of node 2 (null). Done.

testCases.input: Node: val=10, left=null, right=null. Call print_tree.
Node: val=10, left=Node(5), right=null. Call print_tree.
Node: val=10, left=null, right=Node(15). Call print_tree.
Node: val=10, left=Node(5), right=Node(15). Call print_tree.
Node: val=1, left=Node(2), right=Node(3), 2's left=Node(4). Call print_tree.
Node: val=100, left=Node(50), right=Node(150), 50's right=Node(75). Call print_tree.
Node: val=A, left=Node(B), right=Node(C). Call print_tree.
Node: val=0, left=Node(-1), right=Node(1), -1's left=Node(-2). Call print_tree.
Node: val=7, left=Node(6), right=Node(8), 6's left=Node(5). Call print_tree.

approaches.name: Approach 3: Vertical Order Traversal with First Node Selection
approaches.summary: Generate the vertical order traversal of the tree by grouping nodes by horizontal distance, then extract the first node from each group to form the top view. This involves a traversal to collect nodes and sorting by horizontal distance and level.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Top View of Binary Tree via Vertical Order with First Node Selection
ladders.desc: Complete solution: traverse tree to group nodes by horizontal distance, then extract first node from each group for top view.
ladders.explanation: The top view of a binary tree shows nodes visible when looking from above, meaning for each vertical line (horizontal distance from root), only the node closest to the top (smallest level) is visible. This approach uses vertical order traversal: assign each node a horizontal distance (HD) where root=0, left child=HD-1, right child=HD+1, and track its level (depth). During traversal, collect nodes in a map keyed by HD, storing (node value, level). After traversal, for each HD, select the node with smallest level (first encountered in level-order). Sort HDs and output selected node values. Intuition: vertical lines represent columns; top view picks the highest node in each column. Step-by-step: 1. Initialize map hd_map (HD → list of (value, level)). 2. DFS/BFS: for each node, add (node.val, level) to hd_map[HD]. 3. For each HD in sorted keys: find min level in list, output its value. Time O(n log n) due to sorting HDs; space O(n) for map and recursion. Connection to L1: uses vertical grouping subroutine. Common mistakes: not tracking levels (needed to break ties), not sorting HDs (order matters), handling empty tree. Real code pattern: def topView(root): if not root: return []; hd_map = {}; dfs(root, 0, 0); result = []; for hd in sorted(hd_map.keys()): min_node = min(hd_map[hd], key=lambda x: x[1]); result.append(min_node[0]); return result.

examples.input: Tree: 1 (root), left: 2, right: 3; 2 left: 4, right: 5; 3 left: 6, right: 7
examples.trace: Step 1: Assign HD and level: root (1): HD=0, level=0. DFS: left child 2: HD=-1, level=1; right child 3: HD=1, level=1. For 2: left 4: HD=-2, level=2; right 5: HD=0, level=2. For 3: left 6: HD=0, level=2; right 7: HD=2, level=2. Step 2: hd_map: {-2: [(4,2)], -1: [(2,1)], 0: [(1,0), (5,2), (6,2)], 1: [(3,1)], 2: [(7,2)]}. Step 3: Sort HDs: [-2, -1, 0, 1, 2]. For each: HD=-2: only (4,2) → output 4. HD=-1: only (2,1) → output 2. HD=0: min level is 0 from (1,0) → output 1. HD=1: only (3,1) → output 3. HD=2: only (7,2) → output 7. Result: [4,2,1,3,7].

Step 1: HD and level: root 1: HD=0, level=0. Left 2: HD=-1, level=1; right 3: HD=1, level=1. For 2: left 4: HD=-2, level=2. For 3: right 5: HD=2, level=2. Step 2: hd_map: {-2: [(4,2)], -1: [(2,1)], 0: [(1,0)], 1: [(3,1)], 2: [(5,2)]}. Step 3: Sort HDs: [-2, -1, 0, 1, 2]. Output: HD=-2: 4; HD=-1: 2; HD=0: 1; HD=1: 3; HD=2: 5. Result: [4,2,1,3,5].

testCases.input: Tree: 1, left: 2, right: 3; 2 left: 4, right: 5; 3 left: 6, right: 7; 4 left: 8
Tree: 1, left: 2, right: 3; 2 left: 4, right: 5; 4 left: 6; 5 right: 7
Tree: 5, left: 3, right: 7; 3 left: 2, right: 4; 7 left: 6, right: 8

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Vertical Order Grouping of Tree Nodes
ladders.desc: Traverse binary tree to group nodes by horizontal distance, storing values and levels.
ladders.explanation: This subroutine collects all nodes in a binary tree grouped by their horizontal distance (HD) from the root, with each group containing node values and their levels. It's the core data collection step for the top view approach. Intuition: by traversing the tree (DFS or BFS), we assign each node an HD where root=0, left moves -1, right moves +1, and track level (depth from root). This creates a mapping from HD to list of (value, level), effectively organizing nodes into vertical columns. Step-by-step: 1. Initialize empty map (e.g., dictionary). 2. Define recursive function dfs(node, hd, level): if node is null, return; add (node.val, level) to map[hd]; recursively call dfs on left child with hd-1, level+1; on right child with hd+1, level+1. 3. Start with root at hd=0, level=0. Time O(n) for visiting all nodes; space O(n) for map and recursion stack. Connection to L0: this map feeds into first node selection. Common mistakes: forgetting to pass level, using BFS without tracking HD properly, not handling duplicates in map (should append). Real code pattern: def verticalGroups(root): hd_map = {}; def dfs(node, hd, level): if not node: return; if hd not in hd_map: hd_map[hd] = []; hd_map[hd].append((node.val, level)); dfs(node.left, hd-1, level+1); dfs(node.right, hd+1, level+1); dfs(root, 0, 0); return hd_map.

examples.trace: Step 1: Start dfs(1,0,0): add (1,0) to map[0] → {0: [(1,0)]}. Step 2: dfs left child 2 with hd=-1, level=1: add (2,1) to map[-1] → {-1: [(2,1)], 0: [(1,0)]}. Step 3: dfs right child 3 with hd=1, level=1: add (3,1) to map[1] → {-1: [(2,1)], 0: [(1,0)], 1: [(3,1)]}. Return map.

testCases.input: Tree: 1, left: 2, right: 3; 2 left: 4, right: 5; 3 left: 6, right: 7

ladders.title: Core Logic — Selecting First Node from Each Vertical Group
ladders.desc: Given groups of nodes by horizontal distance, pick the node with smallest level from each group.
ladders.explanation: This is the central insight: after grouping nodes vertically, the top view requires the node closest to the top (smallest level) in each group. For each horizontal distance (HD), we have a list of (value, level) pairs; we select the pair with minimum level, and output its value. If multiple nodes have same minimum level (rare, but possible in symmetric trees), any can be chosen (typically first encountered). Intuition: vertical columns may have multiple nodes at different depths; the top view shows only the highest one. Step-by-step: 1. Input: map hd_map from HD to list of (value, level). 2. Get sorted list of HDs (keys). 3. For each HD in sorted order: find min level in hd_map[HD] (e.g., using min with key=lambda x: x[1]), output corresponding value. Time O(n log n) for sorting HDs (O(k log k) where k is number of unique HDs, ≤ n) and O(n) for scanning lists; space O(n) for output. Connection to L1: uses output of vertical grouping. Common mistakes: not sorting HDs (order matters left to right), incorrectly handling empty groups, forgetting to extract value from pair. Real code pattern: def selectFirstNodes(hd_map): hds = sorted(hd_map.keys()); result = []; for hd in hds: min_node = min(hd_map[hd], key=lambda x: x[1]); result.append(min_node[0]); return result.

examples.trace: Step 1: Sort HDs: [-2, -1, 0, 1, 2]. Step 2: For HD=-2: list [(4,2)], min level=2 → value 4. Step 3: HD=-1: [(2,1)], min level=1 → value 2. Step 4: HD=0: [(1,0), (5,2), (6,2)], min level=0 from (1,0) → value 1. Step 5: HD=1: [(3,1)], min level=1 → value 3. Step 6: HD=2: [(7,2)], min level=2 → value 7. Result: [4,2,1,3,7].
Step 1: Sort HDs: [-1, 0, 1]. Step 2: HD=-1: [(2,1)] → value 2. Step 3: HD=0: [(1,0)] → value 1. Step 4: HD=1: [(3,1)] → value 3. Result: [2,1,3].

ladders.title: Building Block — Tree Traversal with Horizontal Distance and Level Tracking
ladders.desc: Traverse a binary tree while assigning horizontal distance and level to each node.
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept involves traversing a binary tree (using DFS or BFS) and computing two properties for each node: horizontal distance (HD) and level. HD measures how far left or right a node is from the root, with root HD=0, left child HD=parent HD-1, right child HD=parent HD+1. Level measures depth from root, with root level=0, child level=parent level+1. Intuition: HD organizes nodes into vertical columns; level determines vertical position. This is foundational for vertical order traversal. Step-by-step: 1. Define recursive function traverse(node, hd, level): if node is null, return; process node (e.g., print or store); recursively traverse left child with hd-1, level+1; traverse right child with hd+1, level+1. 2. Start at root with hd=0, level=0. Time O(n) for visiting n nodes; space O(h) for recursion stack, where h is tree height. Connection to L2: this traversal collects data for grouping. Common mistakes: mixing up HD and level updates, not passing parameters correctly, using iterative traversal without tracking both. Real code pattern: def traverseHD(root): def dfs(node, hd, level): if not node: return; print(f'Node {node.val}: HD={hd}, Level={level}'); dfs(node.left, hd-1, level+1); dfs(node.right, hd+1, level+1); dfs(root, 0, 0).

examples.output: Node 1: HD=0, Level=0; Node 2: HD=-1, Level=1; Node 3: HD=1, Level=1
examples.trace: Step 1: Start dfs(1,0,0): print 'Node 1: HD=0, Level=0'. Step 2: dfs left child 2: hd=0-1=-1, level=0+1=1 → print 'Node 2: HD=-1, Level=1'. Step 3: dfs right child 3: hd=0+1=1, level=0+1=1 → print 'Node 3: HD=1, Level=1'. Done.

examples.output: Node 1: HD=0, Level=0; Node 2: HD=-1, Level=1; Node 4: HD=-2, Level=2; Node 3: HD=1, Level=1
examples.trace: Step 1: dfs(1,0,0): print 'Node 1: HD=0, Level=0'. Step 2: dfs(2,-1,1): print 'Node 2: HD=-1, Level=1'. Step 3: dfs(4,-2,2): print 'Node 4: HD=-2, Level=2'. Step 4: dfs(3,1,1): print 'Node 3: HD=1, Level=1'. Done.

testCases.expected: Node 1: HD=0, Level=0; Node 2: HD=-1, Level=1
Node 1: HD=0, Level=0; Node 3: HD=1, Level=1
Node 10: HD=0, Level=0; Node 5: HD=-1, Level=1; Node 15: HD=1, Level=1
Node 1: HD=0, Level=0; Node 2: HD=-1, Level=1; Node 4: HD=-2, Level=2; Node 3: HD=1, Level=1
Node 1: HD=0, Level=0; Node 2: HD=-1, Level=1; Node 3: HD=1, Level=1; Node 5: HD=2, Level=2
Node 1: HD=0, Level=0; Node 2: HD=-1, Level=1; Node 4: HD=0, Level=2; Node 3: HD=1, Level=1; Node 5: HD=0, Level=2
Node 1: HD=0, Level=0; Node 2: HD=-1, Level=1; Node 4: HD=-2, Level=2; Node 5: HD=0, Level=2; Node 3: HD=1, Level=1

testCases.input: Tree: 1, left: 2, right: 3; 2 left: 4; 3 right: 5; 4 left: 6
testCases.expected: Node 1: HD=0, Level=0; Node 2: HD=-1, Level=1; Node 4: HD=-2, Level=2; Node 6: HD=-3, Level=3; Node 3: HD=1, Level=1; Node 5: HD=2, Level=2

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Assigning Horizontal Distance in a Binary Tree
ladders.desc: Compute horizontal distance for each node relative to the root.
ladders.explanation: This fundamental operation assigns a horizontal distance (HD) to each node in a binary tree, representing its position left or right from the root. HD is defined recursively: root has HD=0; for any node, its left child has HD = parent HD - 1, and right child has HD = parent HD + 1. Intuition: think of the tree on a grid where root is at center (0,0), moving left decreases HD, moving right increases HD. This creates a coordinate system for vertical alignment. Step-by-step: traverse tree (e.g., DFS), starting at root with HD=0; for each node, compute HD based on parent; process children with updated HD. Time O(n) for n nodes; space O(h) for recursion. Connection to L3: HD is one part of the traversal data. Common mistakes: not initializing root HD correctly, mixing up left/right updates, using absolute values incorrectly. Real code pattern: def assignHD(root): def dfs(node, hd): if not node: return; node.hd = hd; # store HD dfs(node.left, hd-1); dfs(node.right, hd+1); dfs(root, 0).

examples.output: Node 1: HD=0; Node 2: HD=-1; Node 3: HD=1
examples.trace: Step 1: Start dfs(1,0): set node 1 HD=0. Step 2: dfs left child 2: hd=0-1=-1 → set node 2 HD=-1. Step 3: dfs right child 3: hd=0+1=1 → set node 3 HD=1. Done.

examples.output: Node 1: HD=0; Node 2: HD=-1; Node 4: HD=-2; Node 3: HD=1
examples.trace: Step 1: dfs(1,0): node 1 HD=0. Step 2: dfs(2,-1): node 2 HD=-1. Step 3: dfs(4,-2): node 4 HD=-2. Step 4: dfs(3,1): node 3 HD=1. Done.

testCases.expected: Node 10: HD=0; Node 5: HD=-1; Node 15: HD=1
Node 1: HD=0; Node 2: HD=-1; Node 4: HD=-2; Node 3: HD=1
Node 1: HD=0; Node 2: HD=-1; Node 3: HD=1; Node 5: HD=2
Node 1: HD=0; Node 2: HD=-1; Node 4: HD=0; Node 3: HD=1
Node 1: HD=0; Node 2: HD=-1; Node 4: HD=-2; Node 5: HD=0; Node 3: HD=1
Node 1: HD=0; Node 2: HD=-1; Node 4: HD=-2; Node 3: HD=1; Node 5: HD=0

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Binary Tree Node Structure and Recursive Traversal
ladders.desc: Understand binary tree node representation and basic recursive traversal.
ladders.explanation: This is the simplest prerequisite: a binary tree is a hierarchical data structure where each node has at most two children: left and right. Each node typically stores a value (e.g., integer) and references to children. Recursive traversal visits all nodes by recursively processing the root, then left subtree, then right subtree (pre-order). Intuition: a tree can be defined recursively: a tree is empty or consists of a root with left and right subtrees. Traversal exploits this by handling the root and delegating to subtrees. Step-by-step: define Node class with val, left, right; define function traverse(node): if node is null, return; process node.val; traverse(node.left); traverse(node.right). Time O(n) for n nodes; space O(h) for recursion stack. Connection to L4: this traversal is extended with HD. Common mistakes: forgetting base case (null check), infinite recursion, not understanding tree structure. Real code pattern: class Node: def __init__(self, val): self.val = val; self.left = None; self.right = None. def traverse(root): if not root: return; print(root.val); traverse(root.left); traverse(root.right).

examples.trace: Step 1: traverse(1): node not null, print 1. Step 2: traverse(left child 2): print 2. Step 3: traverse(left of 2): null, return. Step 4: traverse(right of 2): null, return. Step 5: traverse(right child 3): print 3. Step 6: traverse(left of 3): null, return. Step 7: traverse(right of 3): null, return. Output order: 1,2,3.

Step 1: traverse(1): print 1. Step 2: traverse(2): print 2. Step 3: traverse(4): print 4. Step 4: traverse(left of 4): null. Step 5: traverse(right of 4): null. Step 6: traverse(right of 2): null. Step 7: traverse(3): print 3. Step 8: traverse(left of 3): null. Step 9: traverse(right of 3): null. Output: 1,2,4,3.
